key_points: NHS Scotland provides a standardised inguinal hernia pathway for clinical and patient decision making.
Pathway includes Active Clinical Referral Triage (ACRT) and an Opt-In route for surgery.
Open-ended opt-in can reduce need for GP re-referral where symptoms evolve.
Supports shared decision making and access to non-mesh alternatives where clinically appropriate.

thresholds.escalation_trigger: symptom progression or patient choice for surgery

key_points: In symptomatic patients not fit for / not wishing surgery, conservative management options include advice, analgesia, and consideration of truss.
Unilateral groin pain alone commonly has non-hernia causes; hernia rarely presents with pain alone.
Primary-care ultrasound requested solely to find hernia is generally discouraged due to over-diagnosis risk and low utility.

thresholds.imaging_threshold: use imaging selectively; avoid routine GP-requested US for pain-only presentations

key_points: Primary care referrals include male and female patients with inguinal or femoral hernias.
Patients with acute concerning symptoms should be directed to emergency care (A&E).
Route for referral is explicit (SCI Gateway to Borders General Surgery).
Primary care should treat suspected strangulated or obstructed inguinal hernia as emergency referral.
Irreducible/partially reducible inguinal hernias and all groin hernias in women are urgent referrals.
Symptomatic inguinal hernia or clinical uncertainty generally merits routine surgical referral.
Minimally symptomatic/asymptomatic reducible male inguinal hernia may be managed conservatively.
Diagnostic imaging should not routinely be arranged in primary care for typical cases.

thresholds.urgent: female groin hernia; irreducible/partially reducible hernia
thresholds.routine: symptomatic reducible male hernia or diagnostic uncertainty

key_points: Watchful waiting is safe for minimally symptomatic inguinal hernia in appropriately selected adults.
Recommendation explicitly states all suspected groin hernias in women should be urgent referrals.
Patients should receive safety-net advice on symptoms of incarceration/strangulation requiring urgent review.

thresholds.urgent_women: all suspected groin hernias in women

key_points: Watchful waiting suggested for asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic male inguinal hernia (and pregnant females with groin swelling).
Acutely incarcerated/strangulated hernia requires emergency department referral.
Pathway stresses comorbidity optimisation before elective surgery where indicated.
No current SIGN inguinal-hernia-specific guideline found on guideline index.
CKS source is in-scope but extraction returned licensing/access page rather than topic content in this environment.

thresholds.note: NICE CKS should be checked directly in UK-access environment during clinical sign-off

cross_source_consensus: Emergency escalation for suspected strangulation/obstruction is consistent across sources.
Women with suspected groin hernia should be referred urgently due to higher femoral-hernia risk profile.
Watchful waiting is acceptable for selected minimally symptomatic reducible inguinal hernia (usually men).
Primary care imaging is not routinely needed in clinically obvious inguinal hernia; use selectively in diagnostic uncertainty.
material_conflicts: Local commissioning criteria for elective repair access can vary by region/board; pathway should keep threshold language while deferring local eligibility paperwork details.
Some pathway documents are operational/older (e.g., RCS commissioning guide publication year) but direction of travel is concordant with newer EBI/GIRFT/Right Decisions statements.
